title: Jaywalking in Berlin - Quick Summary
items: **Crossing on red is a real rule, not a rumor.** Germany's StVO (road traffic law) tells pedestrians to use signaled crossings and follow the light, even on an empty street.
**The fine is small but real: EUR 5 to EUR 10.** It goes up fast if your crossing forces a driver to brake or causes an accident.

**A Zebrastreifen works differently from an Ampel.** At a marked zebra crossing with no light, pedestrians already have the right of way. At a signaled crossing, the light overrides how empty the road looks.
**Berliners wait even with no cars because of Vorbildfunktion**, the idea that adults should set the example for any child who might be watching.
**The social cost is bigger than the legal one.** You are far more likely to get a stare or a comment than an actual ticket, especially near children or a stroller.
**My rule of thumb:** if a local next to you is waiting, wait with them. It costs fifteen seconds on a walking day full of intersections.
